name: JavaScript Runtime and Tooling — additive questions
description: Question expansion derived from the attached source files; reuses existing repository topic IDs and defines only missing concepts.

topics.name: JavaScript Module Bundling
topics.description: ES modules, dependency graphs, transpilation, bundling, code splitting, and tree shaking.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

ES modules, dependency graphs, transpilation, bundling, code splitting, and tree shaking.

Build tooling transforms a module graph into browser-ready assets while optimizing development and delivery.

## What problem does it solve?

Confusing package managers, transpilers, type checkers, and bundlers leads to poor diagnosis of build failures.

## How it works

A bundler follows imports, creates lazy chunks, transforms syntax, and emits hashed assets.

- Static imports form a module graph
- Transpilers transform syntax or types; bundlers combine and optimize modules and assets
- Code splitting and tree shaking can reduce initial delivery

For the interview question "Explaining the frontend build pipeline", a complete answer should cover three things: Describes following imports and producing assets. Distinguishes installation, transformation, checking, and bundling. Covers splitting, shaking, hashing, or minification.

## Common mistakes

- Calling every frontend tool a bundler
- Assuming tree shaking ignores module side effects

## Key terms

- module graph — Dependencies connected through imports
- tree shaking — Removal of provably unused exports
- code splitting — Producing multiple loadable chunks

topics.name: Git staging and history repair
topics.description: Working tree, index, commits, reset, and amend when correcting local mistakes.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Working tree, index, commits, reset, and amend when correcting local mistakes.

Git moves changes through working tree, index, and history; repair commands choose which layers to rewrite.

## What problem does it solve?

Using reset or amend without understanding scope can discard work or rewrite shared history.

## How it works

amend replaces the latest local commit; reset moves the branch pointer with soft, mixed, or hard effects.

- The working tree contains current files and the index stages the next snapshot
- A commit records the staged snapshot
- amend replaces the last commit; reset moves a branch pointer and may alter index or files

For the interview question "Correcting Git history safely", a complete answer should cover three things: Separates working tree, index, and commits. Explains replacing a commit versus moving a pointer. Warns against rewriting commits used by others.

## Common mistakes

- Treating reset --hard as harmless unstaging
- Rewriting already shared commits without coordination

## Key terms

- working tree — Current checked-out files
- index — The staged next snapshot
- amend — Replacement of the latest commit

questions.title: Applying Script loading: normal, defer, and async in practice
questions.prompt: A dependency-sensitive application bundle normally uses defer; an independent analytics script may use async. Would you keep this approach? Explain the trade-offs, likely failure modes, and how you would verify it.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains normal scripts
expectedPoints.description: Describes parser blocking behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Explains defer
expectedPoints.description: States parallel download, post-parse execution, and preserved order.

expectedPoints.label: Explains async
expectedPoints.description: States immediate execution when ready and no order guarantee.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Script loading: normal, defer, and async
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Saying async and defer are equivalent because both download in parallel." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Explains normal scripts
expectedPoints.description: Describes parser blocking behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Explains defer
expectedPoints.description: States parallel download, post-parse execution, and preserved order.

expectedPoints.label: Explains async
expectedPoints.description: States immediate execution when ready and no order guarantee.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Using async for scripts that depend on each other or on a predictable execution order?

questions.title: Applying JavaScript this binding in practice
questions.prompt: Passing user.printName directly as a callback loses user as the receiver unless it is bound or wrapped. What conclusion would you draw, which assumptions matter, and how would you validate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Uses call-site reasoning
expectedPoints.description: Explains that invocation style normally determines this.

expectedPoints.label: Covers explicit and constructor binding
expectedPoints.description: Mentions call/apply/bind and new.

expectedPoints.label: Explains arrow behavior
expectedPoints.description: States that arrows capture lexical this and have no own binding.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about JavaScript this binding
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Assuming this always points to the object where the function was defined." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Uses call-site reasoning
expectedPoints.description: Explains that invocation style normally determines this.

expectedPoints.label: Covers explicit and constructor binding
expectedPoints.description: Mentions call/apply/bind and new.

expectedPoints.label: Explains arrow behavior
expectedPoints.description: States that arrows capture lexical this and have no own binding.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Using an arrow as an object method when dynamic receiver binding is required?

questions.title: Applying Scope, closures, and the temporal dead zone in practice
questions.prompt: Callbacks created in a var loop share one function-scoped variable; let creates a separate binding per iteration. What conclusion would you draw, which assumptions matter, and how would you validate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Defines closure precisely
expectedPoints.description: Mentions retained access to lexical bindings.

expectedPoints.label: Explains var behavior
expectedPoints.description: Describes function scope and a shared loop binding.

expectedPoints.label: Explains let behavior
expectedPoints.description: Describes block scope and a fresh binding per iteration.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Scope, closures, and the temporal dead zone
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Describing a closure as merely a nested function without mentioning captured bindings." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Defines closure precisely
expectedPoints.description: Mentions retained access to lexical bindings.

expectedPoints.label: Explains var behavior
expectedPoints.description: Describes function scope and a shared loop binding.

expectedPoints.label: Explains let behavior
expectedPoints.description: Describes block scope and a fresh binding per iteration.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Saying let and const are not hoisted; they are hoisted but inaccessible before initialization?

questions.title: Applying Event loop and asynchronous scheduling in practice
questions.prompt: After the current stack, queued Promise callbacks run before a setTimeout callback because microtasks are drained first. What conclusion would you draw, which assumptions matter, and how would you validate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains synchronous stack execution
expectedPoints.description: States that the current stack completes first.

expectedPoints.label: Explains microtask priority
expectedPoints.description: Places Promise callbacks before timers after the stack.

expectedPoints.label: Explains later tasks and rendering
expectedPoints.description: Describes timer or event tasks and a possible render between turns.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Event loop and asynchronous scheduling
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Saying asynchronous JavaScript means multiple JavaScript statements run simultaneously on one thread." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Explains synchronous stack execution
expectedPoints.description: States that the current stack completes first.

expectedPoints.label: Explains microtask priority
expectedPoints.description: Places Promise callbacks before timers after the stack.

expectedPoints.label: Explains later tasks and rendering
expectedPoints.description: Describes timer or event tasks and a possible render between turns.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Assuming a zero-millisecond timer runs immediately?

questions.title: Applying Promise chains and error propagation in practice
questions.prompt: A final catch handles earlier thrown errors; returning a recovery value makes the next step fulfilled. What conclusion would you draw, which assumptions matter, and how would you validate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains returned values
expectedPoints.description: Connects callback results to the next Promise.

expectedPoints.label: Explains rejection propagation
expectedPoints.description: States that thrown errors travel until catch.

expectedPoints.label: Uses finally correctly
expectedPoints.description: Separates cleanup from recovery.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Promise chains and error propagation
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Starting async work inside then without returning its Promise." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Explains returned values
expectedPoints.description: Connects callback results to the next Promise.

expectedPoints.label: Explains rejection propagation
expectedPoints.description: States that thrown errors travel until catch.

expectedPoints.label: Uses finally correctly
expectedPoints.description: Separates cleanup from recovery.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Using finally as if it were the main error-recovery step?

questions.title: Applying Generator functions and iterables in practice
questions.prompt: A generator can traverse a large structure one item at a time without allocating a complete result array. What conclusion would you draw, which assumptions matter, and how would you validate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains lazy execution
expectedPoints.description: States that values are produced on demand.

expectedPoints.label: Explains next and yield
expectedPoints.description: Describes pause, resume, value, and done.

expectedPoints.label: Gives a suitable use case
expectedPoints.description: Mentions traversal, streaming, or a large sequence.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?
